title: Your Agents Start From Zero Every Morning
description: A live demo showing why the next wave of AI isn't about faster answers. It's about compounding intelligence across your software development lifecycle.
breadcrumbs: Your Agents Start From Zero Every Morning
content: Your senior engineers remember the outage caused by an unbounded delete in production. Your product managers remember the customer who churned because a destructive action had no undo. Your designers remember the interaction pattern that finally cut support tickets in half. That institutional knowledge is what makes your team your team. And none of it exists inside the agents your team is starting to rely on. The Fishbowl Everyone's agents are goldfish. Every lap around the bowl is brand new to them. They don't know what your team learned last sprint. They don't know why you stopped doing synchronous deletes above 10,000 records. They don't know that your compliance team requires audit trails on every destructive operation. So the ritual begins: explain the problem, get an answer, notice it missed critical context, prompt again, correct the model, paste in more files, prompt again, ask for edge cases, prompt again, ask for tests, prompt again. Tomorrow, start over. For junior developers, this is actively dangerous. Agents produce confident, polished answers that look production-ready but silently miss the constraints that only come from experience. Scale limits. Failure modes. Security boundaries. Without those guardrails surfacing automatically, junior engineers ship code that works in demos and breaks under load. Agents can write code. What they can't do is remember why your team stopped writing it that way. The Demo The video above shows a real run on a deliberately ordinary task: implement backend logic for deleting a folder from a mobile app. Anyone who's shipped agents on LangGraph, OpenAI Agents SDK, or CrewAI has seen this exact output. Delete the folder, delete the files, return success. Same answer on day one as day three hundred. The second pass is different. Before generating code, Hyperstruck retrieves learnings from previous engineering tasks and applies them to the current plan. In this case, something like: > Pitfall retrieved: "Unbounded bulk deletes on large tables cause extended locks and timeouts. During a previous cleanup task, a DELETE without a row limit locked the table for 12 minutes. Estimate row count first and batch if above threshold." That's a real lesson from a real prior task, surfaced because the system recognised the current work involves a destructive bulk operation. Not because someone prompted for it. The result: the second-pass implementation includes a preflight row count estimate, bounded batch deletion above a threshold, background job handoff for large folders, ownership verification, audit trail logging, and test coverage for failure paths. None of that was in the prompt. All of it came from accumulated experience. This isn't a markdown file that grows until it's noise, or a vector store you dump everything into and hope the model figures it out. The hard part was never storing lessons. It's knowing which one matters right now, for this task, at this stage of the work. Pay attention to the gap between the first pass and the second. The first is what you get from any agent today. The second is what you get when the agent has actually learned from prior work. What It Won't Do Even on day one, the reasoning engine produces better output than a standard agent. The quality floor is higher before any learnings exist. But the real compounding starts after a few tasks, when the system has context from your actual work to draw on. It doesn't help with trivial tasks. If you already know the answer, you don't need a reasoning pass. Straightforward formatting changes, copy edits, and small UI fixes are better handled directly. And it doesn't replace your engineers. It encodes their judgment so it doesn't have to live exclusively in their heads. The goal is to make "what the senior engineer would have caught" available to every agent, on every task. Where This Goes The first wave of AI made it easier to generate. The next wave has to make it easier to improve. Today, the best teams run on informal institutional memory. The senior engineer who has seen every failure mode. The product manager who remembers what customers actually need. The reliability engineer who knows what breaks at scale. It works until someone is on holiday, switches teams, or leaves. Hyperstruck gives that institutional memory a system. Software development is where we're starting. The same architecture works anywhere people and agents need to reason, act, learn, and get better over time. This isn't AI that responds. It's AI that compounds.
keywords: Your Agents Start From Zero Every Morning A live demo showing why the next wave of AI isn't about faster answers. It's about compounding intelligence across your software development lifecycle.

title: Why Your AI Evaluator Is Lying to You
description: When you ask an LLM to score quality on a scale, it secretly uses three buckets. The fix: stop asking models to judge and start asking them to extract evidence.
breadcrumbs: Why Your AI Evaluator Is Lying to You
content: You built an evaluation pipeline. Your agent does something, then a second LLM scores the output on a few dimensions. Goal satisfaction, correctness, groundedness. Each on a 0 to 1 scale. You check if the scores pass a threshold. If they don't, the agent retries. It looks rigorous. It feels like quality control. It's lying to you. The Empty Band We built exactly this pipeline. A milestone reflector that evaluates each phase of a multi-step plan. Three dimensions, continuous scores, thresholds for pass/fail. The verdict cascades through seven downstream decisions: retry logic, revision dispatch, regression detection, stagnation checks, degradation tracking. We benchmarked it across 42 synthetic cases, 3 runs each. 126 total evaluations. Here's what the score distribution looked like for goal satisfaction: | Score Range | What It Means | How Often It Appeared | |-------------|---------------|----------------------| | 0.8 - 1.0 | Strong pass | 53 times | | 0.6 - 0.8 | Pass | 0 times | | 0.3 - 0.6 | Borderline | 52 times | | 0.0 - 0.3 | Fail | 21 times | The pass band was completely empty. Not sometimes empty. Not mostly empty. Zero scores out of 126 evaluations. The model jumped from "everything is great" straight to "there are issues", skipping the entire middle range. This wasn't a prompt problem. We tested 8 models across Groq, OpenAI, and Google. The pattern was universal. When you ask an LLM to rate quality on a multi-point scale, it collapses the scale into three effective buckets: good, bad, and terrible. The nuanced middle disappears. Your evaluation pipeline has a dead zone. Cases that deserve a "mostly good" score land in either "perfect" or "has problems", and your retry logic makes the wrong call either way. Why This Happens LLMs are trained on human preferences, and humans are bad at Likert scales too. When you ask someone to rate a restaurant 1 to 5, most answers cluster at 1, 3, and 5. The same cognitive tendency shows up in language models: they avoid "hedging" intermediate categories. Fully met or partially met. All correct or significant errors. The model doesn't want to commit to "mostly met" because that requires a precise judgment about degree, and precision is exactly what these models lack for evaluative tasks. This matters because the middle is where the interesting cases live. The milestone where 3 out of 4 API calls succeeded but the fourth timed out. The research phase that gathered good data but missed one source. The analysis that's correct but not deep enough. These are the cases where the retry/continue decision actually matters, and the model can't distinguish them. The Fix: Extract, Don't Judge The insight came from an asymmetry in our benchmarks. The same model that collapsed quality scores into three buckets achieved 100% accuracy on focused extraction tasks. Error classification, tool argument resolution, deliverable identification. When you ask the model to answer a specific structured question, it's reliable. When you ask it to weigh multiple dimensions and produce a calibrated number, it's not. So we stopped asking for judgment and started asking for evidence. One LLM call extracts structured facts: which deliverables were completed, which claims are verified against tool evidence, which steps failed and why. The model answers focused questions it's good at. Then code computes the scores deterministically from the evidence. Before: "Rate goal satisfaction 0-1" -> model says 0.95 or 0.50, never 0.75 After: "Which deliverables were completed?" -> model says 2 of 3 -> code computes 0.70 The scores now span the full range because they're arithmetic, not vibes. A deliverable count of 2 out of 3 always produces the same score. The model's job is to count correctly, not to feel the right number. Band agreement went from 72% to 89%. Verdict accuracy went from 75% to 95%. False positive rate went from 20% to 0%. Latency dropped 37% because we eliminated one of the two LLM calls. Binary Questions Beat Multi-Way Classification The same pattern extends to nuance. We needed to capture subtlety that simple counts can't express: conflicting evidence from different tools, vague outputs that technically address the goal but say nothing useful, deliverables that are mostly done versus barely started. Multi-way classification fails here for the same reason multi-point scoring fails. Ask the model "is this deliverable fully met, mostly met, partially met, barely addressed, or not addressed?" and it collapses to three options. Ask it a yes/no question and it's reliable. So we added binary quality signals: Is the output substantive? Yes or no. Do different tool sources conflict? Yes or no. Was most of the work completed? Yes or no. Does this failure match a known limitation? Yes or no. Each signal is a focused binary decision the model handles with near-perfect accuracy. The scoring formulas combine them into nuanced scores that cover the full quality spectrum. The model never has to produce a number or choose from a five-point scale. It just answers simple questions. The Pattern This generalises beyond our use case. If you're building any LLM evaluation pipeline, the principle is: Decompose judgment into extraction plus rules. The LLM extracts structured evidence. Code applies the judgment. The LLM answers "what happened?" and code answers "how good was it?" This works because extraction is a convergent task (there's a right answer the model can find) while scoring is a divergent task (the model has to invent a number that matches your calibration). Models are good at convergent tasks and unreliable at divergent ones. The practical checklist: Never ask for a score. Ask for facts. Counts, statuses, verifications, binary signals. Split claim verification from scoring. Have the model check each claim against evidence individually. Compute correctness from the verification results. Use binary signals for nuance. "Is most of the work done?" is more reliable than "rate completeness on a 5-point scale." Make scoring deterministic. Same evidence should always produce the same score. If your scores change between runs with identical evidence, your pipeline has a reliability problem. Weight by importance. Not all evidence is equal. Output conclusions matter more than intermediate data. Critical deliverables matter more than supporting ones. Encode this in the formula, not the prompt. The Broader Implication The agent ecosystem is building increasingly sophisticated evaluation pipelines. LLM-as-judge for code quality. Multi-dimensional rubrics for content generation. Constitutional AI for safety checks. Most of these ask the model to produce calibrated scores or choose from category scales. If our results generalise, and we tested enough models to think they do, a meaningful fraction of those evaluations are hitting the same dead zone. The "mostly good" cases are being misclassified as either "perfect" or "problematic", and the downstream decisions are wrong in ways that are hard to detect because the individual scores look reasonable. The fix is structural, not prompt engineering. You don't solve this by adding more examples to the rubric or asking the model to think step by step before scoring. You solve it by not asking the model to score at all. Extract evidence. Compute scores. Let each system do what it's good at.
keywords: Why Your AI Evaluator Is Lying to You When you ask an LLM to score quality on a scale, it secretly uses three buckets. The fix: stop asking models to judge and start asking them to extract evidence.
